text: The surgeon gently sweeps across the supravesicular space, maintaining close proximity to the flap. In the background, an indirect angle hernia is visible. Upon insufflation in Trendelenburg position, the hernia appears empty, indicating it is not incarcerated.
The surgeon performs lateral sweeping motions with scissors, staying close to the flap, while the patient is positioned at seventeen degrees Trendelenburg.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, avoiding the cord lipoma, which is being pulled into view. Gentle cautery is applied to release counter traction, allowing the tissue to fall away. The surgeon progresses to the end of the hernia sac, leaving behind visible fat, indicating the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon addresses the cord lipoma, ensuring better visualization in relation to the spermatic cord after the hernia sac is fully taken down. The cord lipoma is manipulated, revealing its tendency to flop back onto itself. The surgeon carefully maintains proximity to the cord lipoma, aware of nearby nerves, and uses energy application to manage any bleeding.

The surgeon carefully teases the cord lipoma away from the spermatic cord and surrounding structures, using gentle manipulation to separate the lipoma from the base without cauterizing it close to the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon creates a medial pocket by dissecting past the symphysis pubis, extending far below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle.

The surgeon performs high lateral fixation above the myopectineal orifice using two ovicral sutures, aiming to position the mesh close to the iliac vein for optimal curvature. The indirect angle hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon sutures it back onto the flap to prevent complications such as internal hernias or bowel obstructions. Ligating the hernia sac at the base is avoided due to the risk of bleeding and hematomas from its robust blood supply.

The surgeon assesses the reach of the hernia sac and sutures one side of the flap, anchoring it back to the flap. The suture is carried down to the deep internal ring to prevent any sliding that could lead to an internal hernia.
